coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of the role of silver in the photocatalytic hydrogen production reaction using a ternary TiO2/GQD/Ag structure

coredata.dc:creator: Fogaça, Leonardo Zavilenski
Sakaguti, Luiz Gustavo
Inoue Omori, Lucca Sunao
Marinozi Vicentini, Jean César
Batistela, Vagner Roberto
Neves Olsen Scaliante, Mara Heloisa
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen is particularly attractive because its combustion generates only water. This study explored the synthesis of photocatalysts based on TiO2 P25, carbon quantum dots, and silver to enhance photocatalytic performance and selectivity. Characterization analyses using AAS, XRD, FTIR and TEM confirmed the successful incorporation of silver and GQD into TiO2. Additionally, a significant reduction in bandgap energy to 1.66 eV was observed for the silver-containing photocatalyst synthesized via the hydrothermal route. Photocatalytic tests, conducted under UV-C light (18 W) in a batch reactor for 4 h, demonstrated that only silver-containing photocatalysts produced H2. The catalyst synthesized by the hydrothermal method achieved an average production of 26.941 µmol g−1, while the one synthesized by the wet impregnation method reached 25.191 µmol g−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrogen production
Silver
Titanium dioxide

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Methodology for the synthesis of a) T-HT, b) TG-HT, c) TGAg-W, d) TGAg-HT, and e) methodology for hydrogen production.
A) FTIR for synthesized photocatalysts. b) XRD analysis. c) PAS analysis. Tauc plot of PAS for the catalyst: d) T-HT, TG-HT; e) TGAg-W; f) TGAg-HT.
TEM analysis for a, b) T-HT; c, d) TG-HT; e, f) TGAg-W; g, h) TGAg-HT.

A) average production for photolysis and all synthesized photocatalysts. b) Hydrogen production of silver-containing photocatalysts along with their standard deviations. c) Duplicate values for TGAg-W; d) TGAg-HT. e) Hydrogen production rate for silver-containing photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Evaluation of the role of silver in the photocatalytic hydrogen production reaction using a ternary TiO2/GQD/Ag structure

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State University of Maringá, Department of Chemical Engineering, Maringá, Paraná, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State University of Maringá, Department of Chemical Engineering, Maringá, Paraná, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Federal University of Technology – Paraná, Department of Chemical Engineering, Ponta Grossa, Paraná, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State University of Maringá, Department of Pharmacology and Therapeutics, Maringá, Paraná, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State University of Maringá, Department of Pharmacology and Therapeutics, Maringá, Paraná, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen is particularly attractive because its combustion generates only water. This study explored the synthesis of photocatalysts based on TiO2 P25, carbon quantum dots, and silver to enhance photocatalytic performance and selectivity. Characterization analyses using AAS, XRD, FTIR and TEM confirmed the successful incorporation of silver and GQD into TiO2. Additionally, a significant reduction in bandgap energy to 1.66 eV was observed for the silver-containing photocatalyst synthesized via the hydrothermal route. Photocatalytic tests, conducted under UV-C light (18 W) in a batch reactor for 4 h, demonstrated that only silver-containing photocatalysts produced H2. The catalyst synthesized by the hydrothermal method achieved an average production of 26.941 µmol g−1, while the one synthesized by the wet impregnation method reached 25.191 µmol g−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen is an alternative to fossil fuels, photocatalytic redox reactions can split water molecules, producing H2 without emitting atmospheric pollutants, primarily using water and light energy. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is widely used as a photocatalyst due to its low cost, high stability, non-toxicity, high photocatalytic activity, and broad bandgap range. However, its high bandgap energy (Eg), high electron-hole pair recombination rates, and low absorption in the visible light spectrum hinder its application in real-world processes [1].

To overcome these limitations, carbon doping materials, such as nanotubes, fullerene and graphene are used due to their characteristics such as high electron mobility and ability to separate generated electron-holes more efficiently. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a promising cocatalysts because it also has large surface area, and up-conversion effect [1,2]. Additionally, silver can be used since properties such as the Schottky barrier formed at the metal–semiconductor interface reduce electron recombination and facilitate charge separation by trapping electrons [3]. Furthermore, its plasmonic effect and enhanced visible light absorption make it a strong candidate for a cocatalyst. Thus, heterostructures composed of TiO2, GQD, and Ag prove to be highly promising for developing a high-performance photocatalytic material.

Four catalyst systems were synthesized. For all systems, 2 g of TiO2 were used, with two systems being suspended in 50 mL of deionized water, and the other two being suspended in 50 mL of an AgNO3 solution (0.01 M). In one system with deionized water and in the other two systems with AgNO3 solution, graphene quantum dots (GQD) with an average size of 4.9 nm [4] equivalent to 1.5 % w/w relative to the 2 g of TiO2 were added. All systems were stirred for 24 h. Subsequently, all systems containing the AgNO3 solution were exposed to UV-C light for 1 h for silver photodeposition. Immediately afterward, both systems containing deionized water and one system with AgNO3 solution were placed in a Teflon container and subjected to a hydrothermal process at 160 °C for 4 h. All photocatalysts were dried at 60 °C for 12 h and named according to the materials contained and the processes used, namely: T-HT, TG-HT, TGAg-W, TGAg-HT (Fig. 1 a-d).

The experiments were conducted using the system illustrated in Fig. 1e, which consists of a cylindrical quartz reactor (108.8 mL), an 18 W mercury vapor lamp, and an anodized aluminum refractory arc. Initially, 30 mg of catalyst were dispersed in 30 mL of 10 % v/v ethanol as a sacrificial reagent, resulting in a concentration of 1 g·L−1 [5]. The reactor was purged with CO2 for 10 min and pressurized to 1.2 bar. The reaction took place at 25 °C for 4 h, with aliquots collected every hour. The samples were analyzed by gas chromatography, and all experiments were conducted in duplicate.

In order to determine the amount of cocatalysts present in the materials, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) analyses were performed using a Varian SpectrAA 50b instrument, along with thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) carried out on a TA Instruments Q50 system. The crystalline structure of materials was investigated using X-ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns XRD-6000, Shimadzu (Ni-filtered CuK, λ = 0.15418 nm, 40 kV, 30 mA, 0.02° resolution and 2° min−1 scanning speed. The photoacoustic spectra (PAS) were obtained in an experimental homemade spectrometer [6] exciting the catalyst with 800 W lamp with a 13 Hz modulation frequency. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEOL JEM 1400 microscope (40–––120 kV).

AAS analysis revealed silver contents of 1.71 % and 1.66 % w/w for the TGAg-W and TGAg-HT catalysts, respectively. TGA was also carried out in order to estimate the amount of GQD in the materials. However, the incorporation of cocatalysts into the heterostructures significantly altered the water adsorption profile compared to the T-HT catalyst, making it unfeasible to use the mass loss of pure TiO2 as a reference.

The ATR-FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 a) showed similar behavior among the catalysts, with a broad band at 3400 cm−1 and a peak at 1639 cm−1, attributed to O-H stretching and bending, respectively. The 400–1000 cm−1 range indicated Ti-O and Ti-O-Ti bond stretching. GQD presence was confirmed by a shift at 1400 cm−1 in TG-HT, TGAg-W, and TGAg-HT, associated with C-H bonds [7].

The XRD patterns (Fig. 2b) confirmed anatase and rutile phases. A peak at 2θ = 32.25° in silver-containing photocatalysts indicated silver oxide [8]. The photocatalysts visual aspects showed that GQD incorporation gave a brownish hue, silver insertion via the wet method resulted in a darker tone due to silver oxide presence, and the hydrothermal method produced a reddish hue due to surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of metallic silver [9].

PAS analysis (Fig. 2c) revealed that GQD shifted absorption into the visible region (up to 750 nm), while silver-containing photocatalysts showed a significant absorption shift across the visible spectrum. Additionally, it is possible to observe the effect of SPR in the region around 415 nm, characteristic of metallic silver [10]. It is observed that precisely in this region, the photoacoustic signal of the TGAg-HT material approaches that of TGAg-W, inferring a greater amount of reduced silver in the material synthesized by the hydrothermal process.

The Tauc Plot analysis (Fig. 2d-f) indicated that the GQD addition slightly decreased Eg. TGAg-W exhibited a variable bandgap range (1.79–2.24 eV) due to complex interactions inside the material [11], while TGAg-HT had the lowest Eg at 1.66 eV.

Comparing the images obtained in Fig. 3 a,b with the others using TEM results, the presence of larger particles in the catalysts containing silver is noticeable. Thus, it is inferred that the larger particles are silver clusters. Throughout the material, larger cluster sizes are observed for the TGAg-W material, as seen in Fig. 3d. Therefore, the hydrothermal process influences the size of the silver particle clusters.

Hydrogen production occurred only with the photocatalysts containing silver, highlighting its importance in the photocatalytic process (Fig. 4 a). TGAg-W exhibited greater variability in the results, with a standard deviation higher than that of TGAg-HT (Fig. 4b).

This instability can be associated with the in situ reduction of silver oxide (Ag2O) to metallic silver (Ag⁰), as evidenced by the color change of TGAg-W at the end of the reaction, matching the color of TGAg-HT. Additionally, TGAg-W displayed an initial inactivity, whereas TGAg-HT initiated hydrogen production immediately and demonstrated greater stability (Fig. 4c-d).

Fig. 4e presents the average hydrogen production rate of the silver-containing photocatalysts. TGAg-W indicated potential for higher production if the reaction continued beyond 4 h but showed a decline in rate at the end of the first run, affecting its efficiency. In contrast, TGAg-HT maintained a constant production rate.
No byproducts such as CO, CH4, or C2H2 were detected, indicating high selectivity for H2. However, in the absence of stirring, the observed selectivity is based on the CO2 dissolved in the medium.

The presence of silver in the heterostructure was essential in the system under study for hydrogen production, as only the photocatalysts containing the metal generated H2. This effectiveness is associated with the significant reduction in bandgap energy, confirmed by photoacoustic spectroscopy, which showed a decrease from 3.00 eV to 1.66 eV in TGAg-HT.

Among the catalysts, TGAg-W exhibited an initial activation period, delaying hydrogen production. In contrast, TGAg-HT demonstrated greater reaction stability and a higher average production over the 4-hour reaction period (26.941 µmol g−1 compared to 25.191 µmol g−1 for TGAg-W). Furthermore, the absence of byproducts reinforces the high selectivity of the material for hydrogen production.

Leonardo Zavilenski Fogaça: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Luiz Gustavo Sakaguti: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lucca Sunao Inoue Omori: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jean César Marinozi Vicentini: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Vagner Roberto Batistela: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Mara Heloisa Neves Olsen Scaliante: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Brazil’s National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq) and Higher Education Personnel Improvement Coordination (CAPES for financial support, the Laboratory of Adsorption and Ion Exchange of the State University of Maringá (LATI-UEM) for realization of XRD analysis and the Complex of Research Support Center of the State University of Maringá (COMCAP-UEM) for TEM and TGA analysis.
